Nvidia's stock is historically cheap despite all the positive indicators.

However, what investors are really looking forward to is guidance. If recent indications from some of its largest clients have any bearing on that (hint: they do), then Nvidia's stock could be set to soar following its earnings.

I think right now is the perfect time to scoop up Nvidia shares, as the move may be dramatic following its earnings report. I've got a handful of reasons why Nvidia is set to soar, although there are likely many others.

# Nvidia’s $66B Moment: Fiscal 2026 Q4 Earnings Preview
By:
[MarketMinute] 
February 18, 2026 at 11:33 AM EST
![Photo for article] 
As the global financial markets prepare for the most anticipated corporate event of the year, all eyes are on **Nvidia ([NASDAQ: NVDA])**. On February 25, 2026, the semiconductor titan is scheduled to report its fiscal 2026 fourth-quarter earnings, a report that is expected to serve as a definitive health check for the trillion-dollar artificial intelligence industry. With analyst revenue expectations coalescing around a staggering $66 billion for the single quarter, the report comes at a time when Nvidia has moved beyond being just a chipmaker to becoming the foundational "central bank" of the global compute economy.
The stakes could not be higher. One week out from the announcement, investors are laser-focused on the production ramp-up of the Blackwell GPU architecture and the early whispers surrounding its successor, the Rubin platform. As Blackwell remains sold out through the middle of 2026, the upcoming call will likely determine whether the massive capital expenditure cycles of Big Tech can continue to support Nvidia’s vertical ascent or if the market has reached a temporary plateau in the "AI Factory" era.
## The $66 Billion Target: Inside the Blackwell Supply Chain
Nvidia’s projected $66 billion in quarterly revenue—a nearly 67% increase year-over-year—is primarily fueled by the peak production phase of its Blackwell (B200 and GB200) systems. Since their initial rollout in 2025, these chips have become the standard for large-scale AI training and inference. *Nvidia* [*NVDA*] * is set to release its fourth-quarter 2025 earnings report on Feb. 25. Here’s Morningstar’s take on what to look for in Nvidia’s earnings and the outlook for its stock.*
## Key Morningstar Metrics for Nvidia
* [Fair Value Estimate]: $240.00
* [Morningstar Rating]: ★★★★
* Morningstar [Economic Moat Rating]: Wide
* Morningstar [Uncertainty Rating]: Very High## Nvidia Earnings Release Date
* Wednesday, Feb. 25, after the close of trading## What to Watch for in Nvidia’s Q4 Earnings
* Hyperscaler capex plans are massive and accelerating for 2026. We’ll seek confirmation that this spending is still flowing to Nvidia. The firm essentially hinted at $300 billion of revenue for calendar 2026, and we’ll seek validation (if not upside) from here. Upside seems even more likely based on capex guidance from Meta, Amazon, and Google.
* We’d like to better understand Nvidia’s work with OpenAI, which will need to rebound to remain a key partner for the firm in the long run. Nvidia and OpenAI don’t appear to have formally agreed on their previously announced partnership. We’ll seek a status update.
* Memory and hard disk drives have emerged as bottlenecks, and we’ll seek insight into whether this will affect the timing of Nvidia’s shipments and/or data center construction. Nvidia might also provide an update into AI sales into China. China no longer moves the needle for us significantly, but it could provide a near-term revenue boost beyond our current model.

# Nvidia’s $66B Moment: Fiscal 2026 Q4 Earnings Preview
By:
[MarketMinute] 
February 18, 2026 at 11:33 AM EST
![Photo for article] 
As the global financial markets prepare for the most anticipated corporate event of the year, all eyes are on **Nvidia ([NASDAQ: NVDA])**. On February 25, 2026, the semiconductor titan is scheduled to report its fiscal 2026 fourth-quarter earnings, a report that is expected to serve as a definitive health check for the trillion-dollar artificial intelligence industry. With analyst revenue expectations coalescing around a staggering $66 billion for the single quarter, the report comes at a time when Nvidia has moved beyond being just a chipmaker to becoming the foundational "central bank" of the global compute economy.
The stakes could not be higher. One week out from the announcement, investors are laser-focused on the production ramp-up of the Blackwell GPU architecture and the early whispers surrounding its successor, the Rubin platform. As Blackwell remains sold out through the middle of 2026, the upcoming call will likely determine whether the massive capital expenditure cycles of Big Tech can continue to support Nvidia’s vertical ascent or if the market has reached a temporary plateau in the "AI Factory" era.
## The $66 Billion Target: Inside the Blackwell Supply Chain
Nvidia’s projected $66 billion in quarterly revenue—a nearly 67% increase year-over-year—is primarily fueled by the peak production phase of its Blackwell (B200 and GB200) systems. Since their initial rollout in 2025, these chips have become the standard for large-scale AI training and inference. Stocks Refuse to Break Before Nvidia Earnings
David Russell
February 23, 2026
Stocks are holding their ground before the biggest company reports earnings.
The S&P 500 rose 1.1 percent in the holiday-shortened period between Friday, February 13, and Friday, February 20. The tech-heavy Nasdaq-100 also broke a three-week losing streak and had its biggest gain since early January.
Attention now turns to Nvidia (NVDA), which reports earnings after the closing bell on Wednesday. The AI-chip giant has consolidated for almost nine months, causing the broader technology sector to lag. That may boost the importance of the results — especially if they trigger a breakout.
Other companies associated with AI hardware remained strong. Memory-chip makers like Micron Technology (MU) and SanDisk (SNDK) held near record highs. ## Summary

Nvidia, the leading AI hardware manufacturer, is set to unveil its Q4 and full-year earnings, with analysts expecting a 71% jump in EPS and revenue of around $65.9 billion. The company's performance is now closely linked to broader market sentiment, making this earnings call a pivotal moment for investors. Analysts are also scrutinising CEO Jensen Huang's commentary and forward guidance for insights into the sustainability of current AI spending levels. Despite a modest 1.0% gain on Friday, the market is pricing in a potential 7% swing in Nvidia’s stock price around the earnings release, more than double the median 3.2% seen in previous reports. Additionally, Nvidia's recent strategic moves are under scrutiny, including a potential $30 billion investment in OpenAI, the company behind ChatGPT, and a multi-year supply deal with Meta Platforms, which underscores Nvidia's dominance in the AI hardware market. However, the macroeconomic landscape remains uncertain, with potential refunds and new tariffs being implemented. Long-term growth prospects for Nvidia are also crucial for the company, as the demand for AI is expected to grow.
